content.problemStatement:
Chef's favorite part of a string is its prefix (which might also be the whole string).

Let S[1:k]S[1:k]S[1:k] denote the prefix of SSS of length kkk.

Chef decided to give you NNN strings S1,S2,…,SNS_1, S_2, \ldots, S_NS1​,S2​,…,SN​. The iii-th string has value AiA_iAi​, initially equal to 000.

He then gives you QQQ queries, each query is one of the following:


`1 i K x`

Suppose the current number of strings is MMM.

For each 1≤j≤M1 \leq j \leq M1≤j≤M, if Sj[1:K]=Si[1:K]S_j[1:K] = S_i[1:K]Sj​[1:K]=Si​[1:K], add xxx to AjA_jAj​.

That is, add xxx to the value of all strings whose KKK-length prefix equals the KKK-length prefix of SiS_iSi​.
`2 i K T`

Create a new string that equals Si[1:K]+TS_i[1:K] + TSi​[1:K]+T (i.e, the first KKK characters of SiS_iSi​, followed by string TTT), and add it to the current list of strings. Its value is initially 000.

The first string created this way is indexed (N+1)(N+1)(N+1), the second is indexed (N+2)(N+2)(N+2), and so on.
`3 i`

Print AiA_iAi​.
Your task is to print the answers to all the queries of type 333.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the number of strings.
Each of the next NNN lines contains a string composed of lowercase Latin letters.
The next line contains an integer QQQ, denoting the number of queries.
Each of the next QQQ lines contains a query as described above:

`1 i K x`
`2 i K T`
`3 i`




Output Format
For each query of the 3rd3^{rd}3rd type, print the value of the iii-th string.
constraints: Total length of all strings in a test case ≤ 10^5
All strings consist only of characters 'a'‑'z'.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the initial number of strings.
- The next N lines each contain a non‑empty string consisting of lowercase Latin letters.
- The next line contains an integer Q, the number of queries.
- The following Q lines describe the queries, each in one of the three formats:
  * 1 i K X – add X to the value of every string whose prefix of length K equals the prefix of length K of the i‑th string.
  * 2 i K T – create a new string equal to the first K characters of the i‑th string concatenated with string T, append it to the list (its initial value is 0).
  * 3 i – output the current value of the i‑th string.
All indices i are 1‑based and refer to the list of strings as it exists at the moment of the query.
The sum of lengths of all strings (initial and created) over a test case does not exceed 10^5.
content.outputFormat: For each query of type 3, output a single line containing the current value of the requested string.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the 1st1^{st}1st and 2nd2^{nd}2nd queries, the initial value of both strings is 000.
In the 3rd3^{rd}3rd query, we update the strings matching a prefix of length 222 of S1S_1S1​, which is "ab". Since both strings match such a prefix, we add 555 to their values.

Explanation:
In the 1st1^{st}1st query, we add the 2nd2^{nd}2nd string with the prefix of length 222 of S1S_1S1​, which is "ab", concatenated with "de". So, S2S_2S2​ equals "abde".
In the 2nd2^{nd}2nd query, we add the 3rd3^{rd}3rd string with the prefix of length 333 of S2S_2S2​, which is "abd", concatenated with "fg". So, S3S_3S3​ equals "abdfg".
In the 3rd3^{rd}3rd query, we update the strings matching a prefix of length 222 of S1S_1S1​, which is "ab". Since all strings match such a prefix, we add 111 to their values.
In the 4th4^{th}4th query, we update the strings matching a prefix of length 333 of S2S_2S2​, which is "abd". The only strings matching such a prefix are S2S_2S2​ and S3S_3S3​. So, we add 555 to their values.

solution.approach:
We store all strings in a trie. Each node of the trie represents a prefix and keeps:
- an array of 26 child indices,
- its depth (length of the prefix),
- a cumulative value `add` that records how much has been added to *all* strings that contain this prefix (type‑1 queries).
For every string we also store the list of trie nodes that correspond to its prefixes (including the leaf node). This list is built once when the string is inserted; the total size of all such lists over a test case is O(total length) ≤ 10^5.
Processing queries:
* Type 1 (1 i K X): we locate the node that represents the K‑length prefix of string i (it is simply `prefNode[i][K]`) and increase its `add` by X. This is O(1).
* Type 2 (2 i K T): we build the new string as `S_i[0..K‑1] + T`, insert it character by character into the trie (creating new nodes when necessary) and store its prefix node list. Insertion costs O(length of new string).
* Type 3 (3 i): the value of string i equals the sum of `add` of all nodes on the path from the root to its leaf, i.e. the sum of `add` over `prefNode[i][1] … prefNode[i][|S_i|]`. Because the total length of all strings is bounded, the overall cost of all type‑3 queries is O(total length of queried strings) ≤ 10^5 per test case.
Thus the whole algorithm runs in O(total length of all strings + Q) time and O(total length) memory.
The solution works online – queries are answered in the order they appear – and supports the dynamic creation of new strings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(L + Q) per test case, where L is the sum of lengths of all strings that ever appear (≤ 10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) – the trie plus the stored prefix‑node lists.
